narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background, your law school application was carefully evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam based on academic and personal profiles like yours. The model estimated an 82.1% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is why your application was rejected—law schools prioritize admitting students predicted to pass and succeed in their legal careers. The two biggest factors pushing the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing, and your Year 1 decile, also at the lowest level well below average. These early and later undergraduate performance indicators stood out as the strongest influences, signaling to the model a higher risk of bar struggles despite other strengths. On a brighter note, your LSAT score, slightly below the dataset average, actually helped pull the prediction toward passing by showing solid reasoning ability. Your undergraduate GPA, a bit lower than typical, provided a small counterbalance in the same positive direction, while your middle family income bracket nudged slightly toward the failure prediction relative to higher averages, and your full-time student status offered minimal positive influence given how common it is. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your decile rankings are notably lower, putting you in a riskier category, even as your LSAT holds up reasonably well. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted effort—but the model weighs these academic signals heavily. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through stronger coursework or retakes, perhaps retaking the LSAT to push closer to or above average, and consider programs that support undergraduate GPA improvement. Building a narrative around resilience and additional preparation could strengthen future applications. We're rooting for you to turn this around and pursue your legal dreams. Keep pushing ahead.
